speaker: interviewer
text: Lodz Ghetto. You want to give us a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --date here so we know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, this was-- the ghetto was established, as I said, in 1940, around April, in 1940. As the time wore out, wore on, there was a great shortage in food. I was almost always hungry. We were rationed. And we got a card only-- you got a card only if you worked. If you did not work, you did not get the card. And you could get the food only once a week. You know, you got-- and at that time, you got a more or less-- let's say you got a bread-- one loaf of bread for a week. And the bad part about it was that if you really didn't divide the portion that you will have it for the whole week, you were in trouble. And I must, at that time, give credit to my sister-in-law. She took the breads under her supervision. And you are not getting any more today than that. That's it. So that we will have tomorrow the same, and the following day the same, so that the bread will last for the eight days until you're gonna get the new ration. Because neither I nor my brother would probably have the willpower, you know, to eat two slices and put it away. Because as a matter of fact, what we used to do-- what people used to do-- oh, it is not even. Let me just even it out, this side. And then let me even it out on that side. And until-- you evened it out until you didn't have anything left. And my sister-in-law took charge of that. And she said, that's what you're getting. But she was so fair that-- you have to realize if you are hungry, always are, you always are suspicious. Oh, maybe he has a little bit larger. Maybe she has a little bit of a larger pieces.

speaker: interviewee
text: What she said-- she cut it in pieces. And she said, you go ahead and pick. And it might be funny now. But it wasn't funny at that time. Because one scrap of bread meant-- meant a lot. You know? And she said, [INAUDIBLE]. Now, you go ahead and pick whatever you want. So that, that we cannot, that we could not have any suspicion. Oh, you ate more than-- unfortunately, this is, this is how it was. You said she was not very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: She--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was sickly. I don't know. She was a little bit-- she had pneumonia at one time in the ghetto. I know that she had pneumonia. And you have to realize that we did not-- probably the main cause was no vitamins, no food, not real food. And she was not well. I know. She had pneumonia. And she had to lay in bed for several-- for a couple weeks. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you heat this room?

speaker: interviewee
text: The room was-- [LAUGHS] the room was warmed up. We had an iron stove-- iron stove. And-- but we were not the only one. This is how it was. The iron stove was in the middle of the room. And there were pipes ran out until the chimney. And when the stove was on, the pipes were warm. And this is how it was heated. Was it warm? Definitely not. As a matter of fact, we did not get any wood we buy to heat the stove and to cook our meals. We had to bring wood from wherever we could. And this means tearing up fences and wherever we could find, using some of the furniture that we could get by without. Because if we will live, we will have new furniture. And the meantime, you have to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have warm clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have clothing. We had clothing. Probably we were probably fortunate because we had enough fabrics, you know? And we had enough clothing, you know? The main reason was probably with shoes. You know? We did not have too many shoes. But at last part, you know, we, we wore wooden shoes. I don't know if you know what wood shoes means-- wooden shoes. And we wore them. And we used to put on the bottom on the wooden shoes, we used to put pieces of rubber, little pieces of iron, you know, that they should last longer. Because some wood is soft. And it wears out. You know, the wood wears out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get along with all the other people in the ghetto-- the prisoners, really, that-- how did people get along together in this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fortunately we got along pretty good. However, in the ghetto was also several times were hangings in the ghetto. In the ghetto were hangings. As a matter of fact, one hanging was in the tailor shop where, where my brother worked. For some reason, they said that someone stole a roll of thread-- a spool of thread, thread. And because it was Germany, you're not supposed to steal. And either this or they wanted to make an example that the other people should not dare to steal. They hang him. And as a matter of fact, not only that. But they forced out the whole shop to see how he was being hanged. And they built a stage. And they hanged the man. As a matter of fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I did-- no. But, yes, I did. And my brother saw it because my brother worked there. In 1940, in the middle, in the middle of 1940-- July, August-- the hunger got real to us. We were very, very short on food. And we were quite hungry day and night. Day and night we were hungry. At that time, there was a rumor that the Germans are gonna take some people out to work outside of the ghetto. That they will work around outside of the ghetto to do a variety of jobs. I figured that from the ghetto I cannot escape, on account of the barbed wire and to get shot. Once I will be outside of the ghetto, I might have a chance to escape. And I intended of going back-- escape and go back to my parents from whom I didn't hear for the last seven, eight months. I registered to go to that outside work. My brother did not, because my brother was married and he wanted to stay with his wife. And he did not intend of doing it. I registered. And it so happened that they took us, instead of outside of the-- to work outside of Lodz-- they took us to Germany. They took us near Frankfurt an der Oder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now describe how this went. What happened the day that you were to go? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGH] Well, we didn't know. They took us out. And we went through the Baluty Market. We went through there. We went on the buses.

speaker: interviewer
text: As if you were just going to work in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. As if we were going to work in the morning. And they gave us each one a half a bread, you know? And this was quite a lot, you know? And they transported us to the railroad station. And from there--

speaker: interviewer
text: In a truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, that time we were in regular cars-- not in cattle wagons, but in a regular station, in a regular railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you just walk to the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. Walked to the-- from the, from the tracks to the station. And they were, oh, I would say probably around 200 people registered. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All of them men. And they took us to around-- it was in the near of Frankfurt an der Oder, in a little place called Bratz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. Some-- maybe a half a day. It did not take too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they treat you on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't treat us. They gave us-- we still had that half a bread from the morning. And they didn't treat us with food. They didn't give us anything. But the didn't-- neither did they bother us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're on a regular railroad car where you sat in a seat.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a regular railroad car, yes. And all of us-- and we thought, well, it cannot be worse than, than it was in the ghetto-- the hunger, and we got a half a bread. When we came there, in Bratz, our job was to build a camp-- to build a camp for more people. We were supposedly the first one. This was in 1940. This was-- if I'm not mistaken, it was either in August or in September, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: The weather was warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was fairly warm. And we were supposed to build camp. You know, there were pre-manufactured buildings-- you know, barracks. And we helped to build them, you know? And I was there, oh, probably close to two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would sleep-- we build on the barracks. And we-- everyone got a bed. We were, in the barracks we were probably 20, 30 people in a, in one barrack-- something like, like in the military, you know? In a bed, and we got a blanket, you know, and we slept--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay in your own clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We had still our own clothes. They didn't give us any clothes. They-- but they supplied us with a couple blankets and the bed, and a pillowcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not bad. We had-- over there when we made the camps, we had food. We, we had some bread. I would say almost, almost enough to survive. You know, they-- because some of us built the, the barracks. And some of us helped in the kitchen, you know? And some of them dug ditches for the sewers and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I helped build the, the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it barracks out of wood? Bricks? What were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] wood. They were pre-- actually they were pre-manufactured, you know? And we just had to put them together. And we had the supervision by several German men who knew-- tradesmen, you know, who actually were more or less builders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As a matter of fact, those people that they built the thing-- they were very pleasant and very decent. During the nights, during the nights we were watched over by Germans. I don't know if they were SS or they were just regular Germans. But they were in uniforms. They closed all the windows and they shut all the doors.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those people who built the barracks-- they intervened. They complained. You cannot just shut them up. They don't have any air. And you cannot-- what happens if they have to go out during the night? And I know that they got into trouble. Now, remember, this was 1940. And those people-- there were about two or three that they were very pleasant. As a matter of fact, if they-- sometimes, you know, they would leave a sandwich, you know? And they would leave it on the side and walk off, and just wink at us, you know, that we should go and grab it. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were quite-- they were Germans. But they, they were quite decent. They got into trouble with the soldiers in the camp because they wanted that we should have a little bit more air during the night. I know that they balled them out. And the local police came. And you know, they balled them out. And since then, you know, for the remainder-- I stayed in that camp about two months. You know, they were friendly but they were a little bit more reserved. Assumingly they were afraid to go on. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn how to do this, to build this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still don't know how. They told us, bring the board here. And put it up, and hold up here. Put a nail in. Put a screw in here. And I did not-- I don't know if I did it the right way. But I did it. And they supervised it. And as I said, they were very friendly and very-- very civil. After the barrack-- after the camp was completed, they took us and they sent us to another camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a camp-- that camp was called [GERMAN]. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: --actually all those camps that we-- the first one that we were supposed to build-- were supposed to be built for slave labor who were gonna work on the Autobahn. Autobahn is the German well-known freeways that they go. And we were supposed to build the Autobahn. And that second camp that they sent me to, [GERMAN]-- this was an Autobahn lager, a slave lager for the Autobahn. And from then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it from Bratz to [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went by trucks. We went by trucks. By military trucks they took us-- probably within hours. Because they were supposed to be in that, on the, on the, on the vicinity they were supposed to be built.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? And when   we arrived over there, it was already a different situation. In the first camp, we got almost plenty of food. In the second one, the following day that we arrived, we were already sent out to work on the Autobahn. And all we got was a piece of bread and a watery soup. This was the daily sustenance. And what was the work actually? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was supervis-- I had to work at the place-- [GERMAN], Tief und Hochbau, which means they build bridges. They build bridges over the railroad station. And this was a firm Rose or Rosen, I don't remember-- either it was Rose, Rose Tief and Hochbau, or Rosen Tief and Hochbau. And I worked there. Helped build the bridges. I never knew how to do it or what to do. But this is slave labor. If you didn't know, you got a, a few spanks. And you did. All they do is just carry the wagon, and push the wagon, and then shovel the dirt. And this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Manual labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Strictly manual labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a company that was hired--

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, again, this was-- from that camp that we, the second camp, there were several companies that came and picked up slave labor. Let's say, for instance, like I worked for Rose company-- probably about 20 or 25 people. Some other company came and picked up 40 or 50 people. What they did-- I believe it's the same thing as I mentioned before with the [INAUDIBLE]. They contracted. They made a camp. And then they contracted out the slave labor. However, they were supposed to feed us. But they didn't. As a matter of fact, I remember that where I worked they used to say, we are paying good money for you. That they don't feed you and you cannot work-- this is not our fault. You better go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because we complained. We don't have any food. We cannot work. You know? And they said, we cannot help you. We paid good money for you. And this is, this was the bad part about it. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN] And it was very bad. I worked for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you learned German by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, you know, I, I, I understood already. And at home in Poland we spoke Yiddish most often. And German-- Yiddish is more or less a screwed up German, if you want to say. Yiddish, it is-- you know, it's screwed up. And if you know Yiddish, you understand more or less German. However, I picked up German, of course, when I worked there and when I was in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you together with the same people that you had been in this first work camp with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With some of them. Some of them, right? And so that we were split up. That let's say, for instance, that, like, the firm Rose took 20 people. And they wanted those 20 people or the 25 people the same day after day. Because they said, well, we taught them already. They know already how to do. And they know what to do. So they picked the same, the same people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have some buddies by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't-- buddies, maybe. Friends? I would not call. We were just too engrossed, everyone in his thought and how to survive and only on the daily survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around August I found out that the-- let me mention that the firms complained to the camp that the people cannot perform any work. You know? And some of them got real sick, you know, and wounded from the, from, from accidents. They decided-- the camp decided that they are gonna send back some sick people to Lodz. And Lodz will supply them with a new transport of healthy people. Around August or September, I-- it was probably in August-- I registered as sick. And I felt sickly. I wasn't sick, but I felt sickly and tired. And I registered that, as a sick. And-- I believe it was in September-- they sent probably 40 or 50 people back to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back by train and regular train cars. We went back. And, it didn't take long. I believe it would say the same day that we left, we arrived in Lodz, in Lodz. When we arrived back in Lodz, we were not welcomed. Because they were glad to get rid of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Welcomed by whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By, by the Jewish committe-- by the-- well, it was a Jewish clique. There was not a committee. There wa-- we had one Jew-- he was called the Juden alteste. And his name was Chaim Rumkowski. And he was not a-- he was not a saint. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way was he bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way? I will, I will come to it later. I will come to it later. And I will explain about him. Because I know a little bit about him too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so now you're back in the ghetto. When we came back to the ghetto, they were not too pleased to see us. Some of them were quite ill. And some of them were, like, tired and sickly, but still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, let me also mention that in the ghetto they had a hospital. And they took the real sick people to the hospital. And every few weeks, every couple months, the Germans came in. And they picked a [GERMAN] they call it a ratio. And they took the sick people out from the hospital. And of course, everyone knows where they were transported. They were transported into Chelmno. Chelmno was the closest part, you know, to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew this at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know. But we had an idea. We had an idea. Anyhow, when I came back-- punishment because I came back. They gave me two choices where I had to work. The two choices were either clean out the toilets or to work in the cemetery. Now let me point--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you the choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me-- let me--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenreich, or the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Jews. No! No, the Jews. The Jewish bureaucracy, the Jewish chiefs. Now let me point out what I mean by cleaning toilets. In Lodz, there was no sewer. There is no sewer. If it is, I would say, probably about 90% or 95% of the dwellings in Lodz don't have any sewer system. But they all have outhouses. Even where we lived in that building with close to 25, 25 families, they all had to go down, down the steps into the, into the outhouse. The outhouse was an outhouse and it was separated-- three, four stalls. And this is how it was. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you have a little pot in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. Yes, we did. Everyone had it. In the morning, everyone went--

speaker: interviewer
text: And dumped it.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with the pot down. However, let me point out that this was not-- this was how we lived before the war, the same way. If we lived in Brzeziny it was the same way. If we lived in Jezow it was the same. And if we lived in Lodz, it was still the same. Lodz was, as I mentioned, the second largest city in Poland, and the industrial hub of Poland, with textiles. But this is how it was. Now, let me come back. This is what I mean by cleaning the toilets. Cleaning the toilet-- they made barrels, a barrel, and they put the barrel on two wheels. And every two, three weeks, you had to go around from-- it was a full-time employment-- Monday, this house, Tuesday, this house, and pick out the-- empty the toilets into the barrel and put the barrel out to the fields, out to the field and the outskirts, and dump it. They just dumped it on the field.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you can imagine that, of course, there were going to be sicknesses and things. And the people who worked on this-- they smelled two blocks away. You know? Because this is-- it's just normal. It got splashed on their clothing and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, the good part about it is that they got that extra ration of bread. They got an extra slice of bread and an extra watery soup. Now, as I mentioned before, I got the choice either to do this or to do, to go to the cemetery and work in the cemetery. Well, I don't know if I was smart or not. But I picked the cemetery. I picked the cemetery. Now, this was-- I came back. This was 1941. I came back in September 1941. I had to work in the cemetery. And it's true. I got an additional slice of bread and an additional soup, which, believe it or not, it helped. Because at home, I could already slice a piece of bread for my brother and my sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother and sister-and-law were still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Still there. And they still-- my brother still worked in the tailor shop. And my sister-in-law did not, if not mistaken. She did not work. I worked in the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do in the cemetery?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, let me point out that to Lodz ghetto came in people from all-- from half of Europe. From Belgium, from Holland, from France, from Germany, from Austria, a lot of people came in to Lodz. Lodz ghetto was more or less-- it was not a death camp. It was not a dead, a death ghetto. But people were dying there. People came from Germany. They were quite-- fairly well fed. They came at Lodz. We did not have to eat. And people were dying like flies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your job at the cemetery?

speaker: interviewee
text: My job at the cemetery was to carry the dead out. There was-- in the ghetto was one horse. A horse and wagon collected every morning the dead-- some of them in the streets, some of them at homes. At this point, let me point out something else-- how the dead collection was. We were given rations for so many people. Some people, on account of the terrible hunger that we had, kept the dead people overnight, over two, three days, in order to collect the card so that they could get still the ration for the, for the dead people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some people were laying in their rooms dead two, three, up to four days, to wait until on Monday comes the new card route. On Monday, I will get the card. Then he will pick up the dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The collection of the dead-- the, the fellow drove around in a, in a horse and wagon and collected the dead and transported him to the cemetery. The cemetery was a building, a huge building. It was a still cemetery from before the war. It was the same building. And for about the first year, I was working carrying out the dead. Four people carried the dead, you know, on the stretchers. We carried them out to the grave. And at that time, there were probably about 30, 40 dead a day. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of graves were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Officially, legally, the grave was supposed to be 120 centimeters, which is, which is, I would say, probably around-- when I dug later I dug the graves, they were supposed to go above our head. However, it did not work out this way always, because sometimes, you know, the graves were not ready. And there were  dead people. And we had to bury them. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was not according to the rules that they have to be 120 centimeters deep-- the grave. How many bodies went in each grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was one, one body, one body to a grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the bodies treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bodies were treated fairly  nice-- well, not nice. Fairly Decent. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish ceremony at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right! Not-- some of them. It was-- the funny part about it was there was a cantor. The Jewish bodies were put to grave by a Jewish man, who went down to the grave and put them down-- to the same people that they were grave diggers before the war. That they were. They, they still performed the ceremonies of the thing. And if someone could afford-- if someone was in the bureaucracy-- if someone was in the bureaucracy the cantor even said the [HEBREW] and Kaddish. And those people-- some of them were dead that they didn't have anyone. We just carried them out. And they just put them into the grave and, and buried them there.
